coredata.dc:title: Size dependent photoluminescence property of hydrothermally synthesized crystalline carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, S.
Banerjee, D.
Ghorai, U.K.
Das, N.S.
Chattopadhyay, K.K.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, simple hydrothermal synthesis of water soluble Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of different sizes has been reported. The effect of synthesis temperature and synthesis time on the particle size has also been shown. The structures of all the as-prepared samples were studied by field emission scanning electron microscope and high resolution transmission electron microscope. Fourier transformed infrared spectrophotometer analyzes the different bonding present in the sample whereas Raman spectrophotometer quantifies the hybridization state of the prepared samples. UV–vis spectrophotometer gives the variation of absorbance of all the samples with wavelength. Dynamic light scattering study shows the variation of particle size with deposition condition and corresponding zeta potential gives the idea about the stability of the CQD solutions. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the as prepared CQDs were also studied in detail. It is noticed that with the increase of excitation wavelength, the PL emissions for the different samples were red shifted. The results have been explained in terms of the excitation dependent emission, variations in size of the CQD and presence of different functional groups on the surface of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
Photoluminescence property
Particle size

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank the University Grants Commission (UGC), the Government of India, for the financial support under ‘University with potential for excellence (UPE-II)’ scheme . The authors wish to acknowledge the help of CRNN (CU) for providing the HRTEM facility. DB wishes to thank the Department of Science & Technology (Science and Engineering Research Board) ( SB/FTP/ETA-408/2013 ), the Government of India for providing the financial support. SS wishes to acknowledge encouragement of Siliguri Institute of Technology, West Bengal, India. The authors wish to thank Department of Industrial Chemistry and Swami Vivekananda Research Centre, Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur Math for providing optical characterization facility. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Digital picture of all the four samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Particle size distribution of sample S1, S2, S3 as measured by DLS analysis.
FESEM images of all the samples (a) S1, (b) S2, (3) S3 and (d) S4 with corresponding higher magnified image shown inset.
HRTEM images of all the samples (a) S1, (b) S2, (3) S3 and (d) S4 with corresponding higher magnified image shown inset (Also inset d lattice image of S4).
(a) Raman spectra of all the samples and deconvoluted D and G band for S1 (b), S2 (c) and S3 (d) and inset (d) same for S4.
UV–vis absorption spectra of all the samples.
FTIR spectra of all the samples.

(a) Variation of PL spectra and (b) emission wavelength with excitation wavelength for sample S1. Inset 8a the response of the sample under normal UV irradiation. (c) CIE Color mapping. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Variation of PL spectra and (b) emission wavelength with excitation wavelength for sample S2. Inset 9a the response of the sample under normal UV irradiation. (c) CIE Color mapping. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Variation of PL spectra and (b) emission wavelength with excitation wavelength for sample S3. Inset 10a the response of the sample under normal UV irradiation (c) CIE Color mapping. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Variation of PL spectra and (b) emission wavelength with excitation wavelength for sample S4. Inset 11a the response of the sample under normal UV irradiation and inset 11 b fluorescence response of S4 under laser irradiation (c) CIE Color mapping. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The time resolved photoluminescence spectra of sample S1.
Comparison of synthesis time, synthesis temperature, Zeta potential, approximate diameter calculated by DLS method and diameter measured in HRTEM images for the CQD samples S1, S2, S3 and S4.
Different parameters as calculated from Raman spectra.
Parameters associated with decay characteristic of sample S1 (emission wavelength 360nm with excitation 280nm).

head.ce:title: Size dependent photoluminescence property of hydrothermally synthesized crystalline carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Nanotechnology Jadavpur University, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Nanotechnology Jadavpur University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Material Science and Nanotechnology Jadavpur University, Kolkata 700032, India. Tel.: +91 33 2413 8917; fax: +91 33 2414 6007.

Present Address: Dr. M.N. Dastur School of Materials Science Engineering; Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Engineering, West Bengal, India.
Present Address: Department of Basic Science and Humanities; Techno India-Batanagar, Kolkata.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, simple hydrothermal synthesis of water soluble Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of different sizes has been reported. The effect of synthesis temperature and synthesis time on the particle size has also been shown. The structures of all the as-prepared samples were studied by field emission scanning electron microscope and high resolution transmission electron microscope. Fourier transformed infrared spectrophotometer analyzes the different bonding present in the sample whereas Raman spectrophotometer quantifies the hybridization state of the prepared samples. UV–vis spectrophotometer gives the variation of absorbance of all the samples with wavelength. Dynamic light scattering study shows the variation of particle size with deposition condition and corresponding zeta potential gives the idea about the stability of the CQD solutions. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the as prepared CQDs were also studied in detail. It is noticed that with the increase of excitation wavelength, the PL emissions for the different samples were red shifted. The results have been explained in terms of the excitation dependent emission, variations in size of the CQD and presence of different functional groups on the surface of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few decades, carbon based nanomaterials have gained a lot of attention due to their unique electronic, mechanical, physical and optical properties. The different Carbon nanostructures include one dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanofibers (CNF), two dimensional graphene, three dimensional diamond, fullerene or zero dimensional carbon dots (CDs). Of which CDs are basically defined as the spherical carbon particles having dimension between 1 and 10nm. CDs have gained attention of the modern researchers due their unique electronic and optical properties.

CDs are mainly classified into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polymer dots (PDs). Of which CQDs are composed of both the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon. CQDs have many applications as a photocatalytic agent and in optoelectronic devices or in the field of bio-imagining [1–4]. Also there are a number of reports regarding the exciting photoluminescence (PL) property of CQDs, and it has been seen that the variation of CQD size is one of the key process to tune the PL properties of this material [5–7]. CQD is a material of interest for its other favorable properties like chemical inertness, low toxicity and high stability.

There are a number of reports present regarding the synthesis of CQDs from different organic compounds like sucrose, glucose, neem and glycol. CQDs can be synthesized by different top down or bottom up techniques [6,8,9]. For instance, Shcipunov.et.al prepared CQDs from Chitin by hydrothermal technique and Li.et al. reported the preparation of the same by pyrolytic synthesis technique [10,11]. CQDs can also be prepared from carbon nanotubes, graphene and from different natural resources like grass and leaves etc. However, most of the synthesis techniques often require use of toxic materials and multiple complex steps and they have low quantum yield (QY). Sahu.et.al reported the preparation of CQDs from orange juice with exciting bio imaging application and the formation involved hydrothermal carbonization [12].

It is possible to synthesize QDs from different semiconductor like Silicon also [13]. II–VI semiconductor quantum dots have shown fascinating PL properties but the synthesis techniques for preparing such QDs often require the use of toxic and hazardous materials. Thus CQDs have advantages over existing semiconductor QDs for their environment friendly, cost effective and high yield synthesis process [14]. From this point of view, CQDs can be a good substitute for semiconductor QDs. Although the optical characteristics of CQDs were investigated previously, there are not many reports regarding the size dependent PL properties of hydrothermally synthesized CQDs. This fact is rather surprising as CQDs have established themselves to be one of the most important electronic and photonic materials owing to quantum confinement, size dependent PL property and its wide band gap. The PL property of QDs is dependent on the size of the quantum dots and the sizes of the QDs are dependent on the synthesis time and synthesis temperature [6]. Thus the size dependent PL property of these CQDs would certainly be interesting to study.

Keeping all these in mind here we have reported the synthesis of the water soluble CQDs from sucrose by a simple low-temperature hydrothermal approach. This simple, high yield approach is capable of producing almost 60mL solution containing CQDs evenly dispersed in single go. The CQDs prepared in this way are stable even after several months of preparation.

The PL response of the as prepared CQDs was studied by varying the excitation wavelengths. The excitation dependent PL properties have been shown and the corresponding CIE color spectrum (chromaticity diagram) were used to predict the CIE coordinates and approximate color of the emitted light from the CQD samples. Most importantly it is seen that the as prepared samples are so stable that the PL property remain unchanged even after several months of preparation.

CQDs of different sizes have been prepared by a simple low-temperature hydrothermal process as described by Sahu. et al. [12]. In a typical experiment certain amount of water and ethanol was taken in a 100mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor with stainless steel jacket. 1.5625g sucrose was added to the solution and stirred for 30min. The pH of the solution has been set to be 5.

The autoclave was then heated in an air oven for different temperature and time and subsequently the samples were left to cool normally. The solutions were centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 45min to prepare the water soluble CQDs. Course particles dissolved in the solution can be separated through filtration.
Sample S1 and S2 were prepared by heating the autoclave at 120°C for 120 and 150min. For the preparation of samples S3 and S4 the reaction time was 150min and temperatures were 150° and 180°C.

It is to be noted that though initial concentration of all the samples was same, the samples S1 and S2 were colorless but S3 was a little yellowish whereas S4 was dark brown in color as shown in Fig. 1.

The as prepared samples were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi, S-4800), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-JEM 2100), dynamic light scattering (DLS) measuring system, Fourier transformed infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR-8400S), Raman spectrometer (Witec, λ ex=532nm) and UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Also the steady state photoluminescence measurement was carried out by Shimadzu RF5301 spectrofluorometer. The time resolved photoluminescence measurements were performed by Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer with the help of time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) apparatus using a 280nmpicosecond pulsed LED and an MCP as detector. Time resolved spectroscopic characterization was examined for 360nm emission at 280nm excitation for the determination of the excited state life time of the prepared carbon dot samples. The emitted color of the CQD samples was approximately predicted with the help of CIE color co-ordinates (chromaticity diagrams).

Fig. 2 shows the distribution of particle size measured by DLS study for sample S1, S2 and S3 with corresponding Zeta potential for all the samples tabulated in Table 1. It is seen that the average particle size of all the samples (S1, S2, S3) is less than 5nm and thus can be considered as quantum dots. For sample S4 the particle size could not be measured by DLS technique because of the formation of some larger spherical particles as can be seen from TEM images shown in the latter section. Here the QDs are basically sitting in the surface of the larger spheres.

The Zeta potential measured simultaneously by DLS technique varied from 0.004865 to −0.0561mV as given in Table 1. The result surprisingly indicates poor stability of the sample contrary to the HRTEM images shown later section where good dispersion can be seen. This mismatching needs further investigation for clarification.

Fig. 3(a–d) shows the FESEM images of all the samples with higher magnified image shown inset. It is seen that the as synthesized CQDs are so small in size that they are almost out of the resolution of the instrument. Still it is seen that spherical particles of dimension of around 10–50nm are well dispersed throughout the field of view.

HRTEM images of all the samples are shown in Fig. 4(a–d) along with corresponding higher magnified images shown inset. It is seen that for all the samples spherical particles with dimension less than 10nm remained well dispersed surprisingly contradicting the result we obtained from zeta potential measurement. The particle sizes of all samples tally well with DLS analysis. Fig. 4(d) shows that for sample S4 larger spherical particles with dimension as large as 50nm were formed. This is the reason why DLS study was unable to predict the particle size of this sample. However careful observation shows that there are smaller particles with size not more than 5–6nm thus belonging to the CQDs attached onto the walls of these bigger spheres. It is thus seen that size of particles increase with increasing temperature. The d spacing as can be seen in lattice images for the sample S4 (for other sample lattice images are not shown here) shown in the inset of Fig. 4(d) matches well with the (0 0 2) plane of the hexagonal graphite (d=0.35nm).

Raman spectra of all the samples have been shown in Fig. 5(a). For the Raman study the samples were prepared by drop casting on silicon substrate. After drop casting all the samples were cooled naturally and finally dried for 12h in an oven. It can be seen that all the samples except S3 show a sharp peak centering around 975cm−1 which is the signature of second harmonic of TO phonon peak of nanocrystalline silicon coming from the substrate. The other two peaks appearing from 1343 to 1604cm−1 are the characteristics of D and G band of carbon materials. It is seen that the sharpness of both the peaks get decreased from S1 to S3 and becomes almost invisible in case of S4. Also it should be noted that the intensity ratio of D to G band can be exploited to determine the disorder present in the sample. The G band is the characteristic of sp2 bonded carbon in all possible forms whereas D band is breathing of sp2 present in rings and not chains. The intensity ratio of I D/I G is the measure of the corresponding parameter present in the sample [15]. Here instead of calculating ID/IG ratio we have calculated the ratio of integrated areas (A D/A G) of these two bands by deconvoluting in origin software to determine the disorder present in all the samples. The deconvoluted spectra for sample S1, S2 and S3 have been shown in Fig. 5(b–d) with the spectra of S4 shown in the inset of Fig. 5(d). It is seen that the spectra for S4 is quite featureless and thus is not suitable for all these calculations mentioned above. Table 2 summarizes the value of all the parameters mentioned above for sample S1, S2, S3 where the full width at half maxima (FWHM) of G band represents the structural disorder only and it arises from bond angle and bond length distortion [16].

Also Raman spectra can very effectively be used to determine sp3 content present in the sample that can be evaluated quantitatively using the relation [17]
(1)
sp
3
content
=
0 .24 − 48
.9 × ( γ
G
− 0.1580 )
where γG is the peak position of G band in unit of inverse micrometer unit. The values of the sp3 content of all the samples are also given in Table 2.

Fig. 6 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of the four CQD samples. It can be seen from the figure that the spectra shows a featureless characteristic throughout the entire scan range giving almost zero absorption except a sharp peak centering around 284nm. This region has separately been shown in the inset. This absorption is the main characteristic of π→π* electron transfer in C=C bonds [10]. It can be clearly seen that the intensity of the peak gradually increases from sample S1 and becomes the highest for sample S3. Also careful observation shows that there is another weak band present in the spectrum centering around 228nm which is the signature of the presence of different polyaromatic chromophores appearing during the synthesis of the four samples of CQDs [10,18]. It can be seen that the absorption of S4 has been decreased with respect to S3 though particle size of S4 is higher than S3. The actual explanation is yet to be known in detail however it can be seen that in S4, small CQDs are attached to the surfaces of larger CQDs. So this decrease in absorbance can be due to the decrease in number of particles for QDs of larger sizes. This has been proposed by Goh et al. for their ZnO nano particle [19]. Also Gharibshahi and his co-worker observed the same phenomena in their colloidal platinum nanoparticles [20] however they remained silent regarding the reasons.

Fig. 7 shows the FTIR spectra in absorption mode of all the four CQDs taken in the wavenumber range 500–4500cm−1 with the resolution of 4cm−1. All the samples were prepared by drop casting on Si wafer as described in the previous section. It is seen that the spectra is almost identical for all the samples with several distinct absorption peaks centering around 600, 900, 1100 and 2900cm−1. There are two other bands between 1400 and 1850cm−1, 3000 and 3500cm−1. Of which the last band is the characteristic of the OH bond [21]. The band between 1400 and 1850cm−1 is the signature of presence of different C–H, C–C and C=O bonds [22]. The peak at 2900cm−1 is the characteristic of different C–H absorption band [11]. The peak around 600cm−1 forms from the Si–O–Si bond of silicon substrate due to formation of SiO2 layer [23]. The peak around 900cm−1 forms due to the presence of Si–H bending vibrations and it again comes from the substrate [24]. The peak at 1100cm−1 is due to the presence of C–O–C bond [22]. For clear observation of all the peaks distinctly, the FTIR spectra of all the samples have been separately shown in Fig. 1 of the Supplementary material.

The size and excitation energy dependent photoluminescence property of all the samples have been studied in much detail. In Fig. 8(a) and (b) PL spectra of sample S1 for different excitation wavelength is shown and the UV response of the same sample under commercially available UV torch (395nm) is shown in the inset. Also Fig. 8(b) shows the variation of emission wavelength (λ em) with excitation wavelength (λ ex). It is seen in Fig. 8(a) that when the excitation wavelength was increased from 240 to 440nm with a gradual increment of 40nm S1 showed down-conversion in PL characteristics as reported by other research groups [22]. More specifically when the λ ex was changed from 240nm to 280nm the emission peak was blue shifted from 331 to 325nm and this blue shift can be due to the presence of sp2 clusters and different attachments trying to reduce the energy gap [6,25]. The highest intensity in the PL characteristic of the sample S1 was obtained when the sample was excited with 280nm.

With further increase of λ ex from 280 to 400nm, the emission peak has been red shifted from 325 to 433, nm with a gradual decrease in intensity as shown. However with further increase in λ ex the PL property becomes negligible as it can be seen from Fig. 8(a). This decrease in intensity has been observed by other groups and the result has been well explained in terms of variation of size of CQD, presence of different trap states and different polyaromatic fluorophores with the skeleton of carbon [26].

The PL spectra of sample S1 has been mapped with the CIE co-ordinates (chromaticity diagrams) and the mapping for the spectra is shown in Fig. 8(c). It is seen from the CIE images that the characteristic colors of the emission spectra visibly changed from light purple, purple, purple blue, blue, deep blue and to light green to when the excitation wavelength was increased from 240nm to 440nm for S1 as shown in Fig. 8(c).

Fig. 9(a) and (b) shows the excitation dependent PL property of S2 with corresponding variation of λ em as shown in Fig. 9(b). The response of S2 under UV irradiation is shown in the inset of Fig. 9(a). It is noteworthy that the samples S1 and S2 were synthesized at same temperature but with different synthesis time. As a result the particle sizes of the two samples S1 and S2 were different and these are confirmed from DLS and HRTEM analysis. This difference in particle size has shown profound effect on the PL spectra. When the λ ex was increased from 240 to 280nm the emission peak was shifted from 336 to 329nm. With further increase of λ ex was increased from 320 to 440nm with the same interval of 40nm the emission peak was red shifted. The maximum intensity was observed for λ ex=360nm unlike in case of S1 where it was λ ex=280nm. Here also the CIE chromaticity diagrams for the S2 have been shown in Fig. 9(c). It can be seen from CIE color mapping in Fig. 9(c) that the characteristic colors of the emission spectra was changed from light purple, purple, blue, sky blue and to light green.

The same λ ex dependent variation of the PL spectra and hence the variation of λ em of S3 is shown in Fig. 10(a) and (b) with corresponding CIE chromaticity diagrams shown in Fig. 10(c). In the inset of Fig. 10(a) the response of sample under same UV radiation (as mentioned previously in case of S1 and S2 has been shown). Here also we can see the marked change and shifting of emission peak with different λ ex. It is seen that with increasing λ ex the sample shows a monotonic red shift from 431.88 to 551.31nm. The value of λ em corresponding to maximum intensity is again at 360nm and thus same as that of sample S2. From CIE mapping in Fig. 10(c), it was observed that sample shined in purple, blue, sky blue, light green to yellowish green when the λ ex was changed.

Fig. 11(a) and (b) shows the same variation of PL property for S4. Here λ ex was varied from 320 to 520nm with the same increment of 40nm. Corresponding λ em were shifted from 432 to 438.2, 503.2, 519, 537.4 and finally to 580.6nm as shown in Fig. 11(b). Here also the maximum intensity was observed for excitation wavelength of 360nm like S2 and S3. The corresponding CIE color co-ordinate mapping is shown in the Fig. 11(c). From the CIE color coordinates mapping in Fig. 11(c), it was noticed that the sample shined approximately in dark blue, sky blue, light green, yellowish green, yellow and light brown when the λ ex were gradually changed from 320 to 520nm.

If the PL properties of S2, S3 and S4 are compared then it can be observed that emissions were observed for S4 for a wide range of excitation wavelength compared to S2 and S3. It can be seen in the HRTEM image that the almost circular CQDs are well dispersed in solution for S4. The synthesis temperature was gradually increased from 120°C to 150°C and finally to 180°C keeping the synthesis time constant at 150min during the synthesis of S2, S3 and S4. It is evident from the HRTEM images that the size and diameter of CQDs gradually increased for S2, S3 and S4 which in turn resulted in the difference in the PL properties. It is to be noted that CQDs can have different chromophoric center of CQD skeleton and different energy trap centers on the surface of the CQD which along with the size are believed to be the major parameters for PL properties [27]. It is also known that the optical properties in solids take place due to activities of hole and electron [28]. It is reported that the emission in the range of 430–446nm (blue emission) is due to the electron hole recombination or quantum size effect and emission in the range of 510nm (green emission) is due the presence of surface defect states [25]. Moreover presence of different functional groups like C=O, C–H on the surface of CQD creates different emissive traps. Certain trap states become active with the change in excitation wavelength resulting in a change of emission as evident in the PL property all the four samples [12]. For most of the samples the emission was in visible range only (400–700nm). So the CQD samples can be used for bio-imaging and optoelectronic applications. Hence, the exciting photoluminescence (PL) property of the CQDs can be attributed to the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon core, quantum confinement and presence of different trap states on the surface of the quantum dots as reported by others [29]. Thus the possibilities of the existence of such additional PL centers in our case also are yet to be investigated. It is to be noted that for sample S3 and S4 in some cases double peaks in PL spectra can be observed which is mainly associated with the double size distribution of the CQDs as seen in the HRTEM images of S4 in Fig. 3(d). where smaller particles are attached to the walls of the bigger spheres. In the inset of Fig. 11(b) the luminescence nature of S4 when it is illuminated with laser light (λ=532nm) has been shown which is not seen in the case of other three samples and thus is not given here. It may seem to one that this is rather contradictory to the result obtain in Fig. 11(a) where it is seen that when the sample is excited with the excitation wavelength 520nm response is flat but for 532nm excitation the sample shows luminescence. However it should be noted that the PL spectra was studied with Xenon lamp whereas the 532nm excitation was given with the help of a LASER lamp. Thus there is expected mismatching in intensity for these two cases. Also it can be seen that when PL spectra of S4 is given separately as in Fig. 2 in the Supplementary material, for λ ex=520nm the response is not zero thus the fallacy is solved.
Also the time resolved photoluminescence measurements were studied for all the samples and the decay characteristic of S1 has been shown in Fig. 12.

The time decay curve was well fitted to a double-exponential decay Eq. [30,31], as
(2)
I
( t )
=
A
1
e x p
(
− t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
e x p
(
− t /
τ
2
)
Where τ 1 and τ 2 are the decay components and A 1 and A 2 are the fitting constants. The details of values are given in the Table 3. The average life time (τ av) is calculated by the following Eq. [30,31].
(3)
τ
a v
= (
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
) / (
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
)

The calculated τ av for S1-Carbon dot is found to be 5.9ns and is well matched with the previously reported result [32]. This short life fluorescence life time is attributed to the radiative recombination of the excitons of the synthesized carbon dots.

The exciting photoluminescence property confirms that the as prepared CQD samples can be used for potential bio-imagining application. Moreover due to stable light emitting and high solubility in water the CQDs can find their application in optoelectronic and Biosensors also.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have been synthesized via very simple hydrothermal process followed by centrifugation. The synthesis process is cheap and does not pose any threat to the nature. The effects of the synthesis temperature and synthesis time on the size and diameter of the CQD have been investigated. HRTEM as well as DLS study confirmed the variation of sizes of the CQDs with different synthesis condition. FTIR spectroscopic studies revealed the different bonds and possible attachments of different functional groups on the surface of the CQDs. Raman study quantifies the amount of differently hybridized carbon present in the samples. As prepared CQDs showed excellent PL property that shows profound effect on synthesis condition thus particle size as well as on the excitation wavelength. The CIE co-ordinates, with approximate colors emitted from the samples as a result of different excitation wavelength (λ ex), have been mapped with the CIE spectrum. The exciting variation in PL property is explained in terms of the structure and different bonds of the carbon dots and the presence of different energy trap states on the surface of the CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the University Grants Commission (UGC), the Government of India, for the financial support under ‘University with potential for excellence (UPE-II)’ scheme. The authors wish to acknowledge the help of CRNN (CU) for providing the HRTEM facility. DB wishes to thank the Department of Science & Technology (Science and Engineering Research Board) (SB/FTP/ETA-408/2013), the Government of India for providing the financial support. SS wishes to acknowledge encouragement of Siliguri Institute of Technology, West Bengal, India. The authors wish to thank Department of Industrial Chemistry and Swami Vivekananda Research Centre, Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur Math for providing optical characterization facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.05.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
